text: The surgeon clears some adhesions to allow the placement of an additional port. Dissection begins close to the abdominal wall, targeting the loose areolar tissue to create windows for further surgical access.

The surgeon carefully dissects on both sides of a loop of small bowel that is partially obscured by omental adhesions, ensuring safe port placement and preventing the bowel from adhering to the abdominal wall.

The surgeon employs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, maintaining proximity to the abdominal wall. Occasionally, the dissection penetrates the posterior rectus sheath when the bowel is tightly adhered to the abdominal wall.

The surgeon proceeds with dissection using blunt techniques, guided by loose areolar tissue, while being cautious of the force applied to prevent trauma. Due to thickened midline adhesions, the surgeon uses a single instrument to take down the adhesions, focusing on the dense omental tissue visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully performs adhesiolysis, searching for hidden small loops of bowel that may be obscured by adhesions. The dissection aims to preserve anatomical planes crucial for subsequent colonic mobilization during laparoscopic colorectal surgery.

After taking down the abdominal wall adhesions, the surgeon addresses the greater omentum, which is adhered to the pelvic cavity and the right colon, causing distortion of the colonic anatomy. The surgical field shows the omentum obscuring the view of the colon, requiring careful dissection to restore anatomical clarity.

The surgeon begins restoring the colonic anatomy by carefully dividing the greater omentum where safe, releasing it from the transverse colon. The transverse colon is pushed down over the liver to expose the transverse myelocolon. Adhesions are meticulously released from the top of the right colon, creating a plane to detach adhesions from the right colon and terminal ileum. The terminal ileum and small bowel loops are visibly hitched up and adhered to the abdominal wall.

The surgeon picks up the ileocolic pedicle and divides it, developing a plane between the retroperitoneal structures and the right mesocolon. The dissection proceeds onto the right-sided fascia of Toldt.

The surgeon carefully protects the duodenum and the head of the pancreas while using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques. After taking the right colonic vessels, the transverse mesocolon is flipped onto the liver to facilitate the dissection and identification of the middle colic vessels. This step follows thorough adhesiolysis and restoration of colonic anatomy, crucial for laparoscopic colectomy.
The surgeon dissects the middle colic artery, visually identifying its division into right and left branches, and ligates it near its origin.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the right colon, with the transverse colon appearing relatively mobile following the division of the mid-colic artery. From the left side of the patient, the gastrocolic omentum is opened, separating the stomach from the transverse colon. The surgeon then moves to the right side of the patient to continue opening the gastrocolic omentum, progressing toward the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs a lateral to medial mobilization of the left colon to properly take down the splenic flexure. The splenic flexure is challenging due to multiple layers of adhesion between the spleen and the colon.

The surgeon divides the bowel at an appropriate level on the descending colon after mobilizing the splenic flexure. The terminal ileum is also divided, and both ends are joined together using an extracorporeal anastomosis technique.
